introduction.text: We are entering the final mechanic: SPLIT. When you have two identical cards, you can pay extra to play two hands. But should you? Today we learn the 'Golden Rules'. There are pairs we ALWAYS split, and pairs we NEVER split. Memorize these, and you avoid the biggest mistakes in Blackjack.

scenarios.title: Always Split Aces (A,A vs 9)
scenarios.goal: Teach the most important split: Aces.

sequence.text: Two Aces. Together they are just 2 or 12. But separate? They are two elevens! This is the strongest move in the game. ALWAYS Split Aces.

finish.text: Look at that! Two hands of 21. You turned a confusing hand into a double victory.

scenarios.title: The Rescue Mission (8,8 vs 10)
scenarios.goal: Teach splitting 8s to save a losing hand.

sequence.text: Two Eights. Total 16. Against a 10, this is a guaranteed loss if you Hit or Stand. You have nothing to lose. SPLIT them to fight with two starting 8s.

finish.text: Incredible recovery. You got 18 and 19. Both beat my 17. You turned trash into treasure.

sequence.text: Two Kings. You have 20. You might be tempted to split to win twice against my weak 6. DON'T. 20 is a winning hand. Don't ruin it. Just Stand.

finish.text: I busted. You win. Simple. Never split 20.

scenarios.title: The Fake Pair (5,5 vs 5)
scenarios.goal: Teach that 5,5 is treated as Hard 10 (Double).

sequence.text: Two Fives. Is it a pair? No, it's a 10! If you split, you have two hands starting with 5 (very weak). If you keep them, you have 10. Treat it like a Hard 10. DOUBLE!

finish.text: Correct. You got 20. Splitting 5s is a rookie mistake. Doubling 5s is a pro move.

scenarios.title: Review: Aces vs Ace
scenarios.goal: Reinforce: We split Aces even against an Ace.

sequence.text: Aces again. I have an Ace too. It's scary, but the rule doesn't change. Aces are too powerful to keep together. SPLIT.

finish.text: Hand 1 wins (21 vs 20). Hand 2 is a Push (20 vs 20). Overall, you made a profit.

conclusion.text: You now know the Golden Rules: Split Aces and 8s. Never Split 10s and 5s. But what about 2s, 3s, 6s, 7s, 9s? That depends entirely on what the dealer has. We will learn these tricky splits in the next lesson!
